entries.document_title: ASIC's administration of the sustainability reporting requirements
entries.summary: ASIC's stated approach to administering the Chapter 2M sustainability reporting regime: a 'pragmatic and proportionate' approach to supervision and enforcement during the transition period, including engaging with reporting entities where disclosures appear incorrect or misleading, and being more likely to commence an enforcement investigation where there is misconduct of a serious or reckless nature, or where a reporting entity fails to prepare a required sustainability report at all. entries.document_title: Treasury Laws Amendment (Financial Reporting System Reform) Bill 2026
entries.summary: The Treasury Laws Amendment (Financial Reporting System Reform) Bill 2026 merges the Financial Reporting Council, the AASB and the AUASB — the two standard-setting bodies already tracked in this case (E-006/E-007/E-019/E-022/E-024 relate to their sustainability-assurance and audit functions) — into a single new body, External Reporting Australia, with an overarching Governing Council and three standard-setting boards covering accounting, audit and assurance, and sustainability reporting. Multiple independent sources report the bill has passed Parliament, with Greens-secured amendments barring representatives of large auditing/consulting firms from sitting on the new Governing Council or its standard-setting boards. Commencement of parts of Schedule 1 is tied to Royal Assent (transitional provisions from the later of 30 June 2026 or the day after Royal Assent; substantive provisions from the later of 1 November 2026 or four months after Royal Assent).
entries.verification_notes: Source URL located and cross-confirmed via two independent search queries returning consistent detail (the merger structure, the External Reporting Australia name, the Greens amendment, the commencement dates) matching this exact Parliament of Australia bill page, corroborated by the bill's Austlii explanatory memorandum and independent accounting-industry press. document_hash remains unset since this sandbox cannot fetch aph.gov.au directly. States that strengthening market disclosure - financial reporting, sustainability reporting and audit - is a key ASIC strategic priority, and that ASIC's surveillance now includes review of mandatory Chapter 2M sustainability reports, taking a 'pragmatic and proportionate' approach to supervision and enforcement in the early years of the regime.

entries.document_title: Additional Budget Estimates, Opening Statement, Senate Economics Legislation Committee, 5 June 2026
entries.summary: New ASIC Chair Sarah Court's first parliamentary committee appearance in the role, delivered to the Senate Economics Legislation Committee's Additional Budget Estimates hearing. Confirms ASIC's administration of the mandatory Chapter 2M climate reporting framework continues, with a focus on Group 1 entity sustainability reports and ongoing engagement with large audit firms on assurance methodologies. States the review of reports for the year ended 31 December 2025 is ongoing, with comprehensive final observations expected in the second half of 2026 - consistent with, but a distinct primary document from, ASIC's already-logged media releases on the same timeline.

entries.document_title: Portfolio Budget Statements 2026-27, Budget Related Paper No. 1.15 (Treasury Portfolio)
entries.summary: The 2026-27 Portfolio Budget Statements flag government-planned adjustments to the Chapter 2M climate-related financial disclosures (sustainability reporting) regime intended to reduce the compliance burden on smaller entities: raising the consolidated revenue threshold that determines which large proprietary companies are captured from $50 million to $100 million, and the consolidated gross assets threshold from $25 million to $50 million (no change to the 100-employee test), which would shrink the population of Group 3 reporting entities. The Budget also foreshadows a further Treasury consultation, with ASIC involvement, on relief concepts including clarifying 'undue cost or effort' (including Scope 3 relief), recalibrating assurance settings proportionate to reporter size/maturity, and clearer boundaries on supplier information requests.
entries.verification_notes: The primary PDF could not be directly fetched (asic.gov.au/treasury.gov.au blocked by the sandbox's outbound proxy, HTTP 403), so this entry rests on cross-referenced search corroboration: the exact document title, paper number, and file path were confirmed via search indexing, and the specific dollar figures and consultation elements were corroborated consistently across five or more independent professional-services summaries (CA ANZ, BDO, Allens, NetNada, Avarni, Accounting Times) across two separate search queries. entries.document_title: ASIC updates financial reporting relief instruments (ASIC Corporations (Electronic Lodgment of Financial and Sustainability Reports) Instrument 2026/59)
entries.summary: ASIC remade three legislative instruments ahead of their 1 April 2026 sunset date. Instrument 2026/59, ASIC Corporations (Electronic Lodgment of Financial and Sustainability Reports) Instrument 2026/59, replaces the earlier 2016/181 instrument and extends electronic dual-lodgment relief for financial, sustainability and directors' reports to entities listed on Cboe Australia (previously the relief applied only to entities listed on ASX/NSX/SSX). Two companion instruments were also remade: 2026/183 (rounding in financial/directors' reports) and 2026/180 (disregarding technical relief). All three now expire 1 April 2031. This is the first Chapter 2M-specific legislative instrument (rather than guidance or media commentary) captured for this case.
entries.verification_notes: The primary ASIC page and the legislation.gov.au register entry (F2026L00362) could not be directly fetched (sandbox proxy blocks asic.gov.au/legislation.gov.au, HTTP 403), so verification rests on cross-referenced search corroboration: the instrument number, full title, sustainability-report coverage, and Cboe Australia extension were each independently confirmed across three separate search queries returning consistent results from ASIC and legislation.gov.au search-index snapshots. The exact consultation/making dates were not independently confirmable via search snippets alone.

proof_of_fact.rationale: Genuine legislative instrument, core facts (number, title, sustainability-report scope, Cboe extension) confirmed across three independent searches; exact dates and primary-text wording not directly read due to fetch blocks.

entries.document_title: ASIC and AASB launch sustainability reporting webinar series
entries.summary: ASIC and the AASB jointly hosted a three-part series of free online webinars, distinct from the earlier in-person workshop series already logged, aimed at smaller and mid-size companies still building capacity for climate-related financial disclosures. The first webinar (16 June 2026) covered an introduction to climate science; the third webinar (30 June 2026) covered GHG emissions accounting - Scope 1, 2 and 3 calculations, the GHG Protocol, and financed emissions. Each session featured University of Technology Sydney (UTS) presentations followed by Q&A with ASIC, AASB and UTS.

entries.document_title: AASB S2 Knowledge Hub - General FAQs
entries.summary: The AASB launched an 'AASB S2 Knowledge Hub' (around May 2025) as a standing implementation-support resource for its own AASB S1/S2 sustainability disclosure standards, structured into General FAQs, Technical FAQs, an Amendments FAQs sub-page, plus guidance and additional resources. The General FAQs sub-page covers foundational application material: what the Australian Sustainability Reporting Standards are, their mandatory application under the Corporations Act 2001, reporting timelines and obligations, and alignment with the ISSB's IFRS S2. This is distinct from the already-logged ASIC audit/review FAQs (E-014) and AUASB assurance FAQs (E-022), which address audit and assurance procedure rather than general standard interpretation, and from the AASB S2025-1 GHG amendment (E-028), which the Hub itself splits into a separate Amendments FAQs sub-page.
entries.verification_notes: Confirmed via two independent WebSearch queries as a genuine, distinct AASB primary resource (issued by the standard-setter itself, not ASIC or AUASB), with content scope independently distinguished from already-logged FAQ/guidance items. The Hub's approximate May 2025 launch date is corroborated by an independent secondary source (xbrl.org); the specific General FAQs sub-page's own last-updated date could not be confirmed since the sandbox cannot directly fetch aasb.gov.au (blocked). Recorded at MEDIUM confidence given the imprecise dating.

proof_of_fact.rationale: Genuine, distinct AASB standard-setter resource with scope independently confirmed via search corroboration; exact sub-page publication/update date and full content not directly read due to fetch blocks.

entries.document_title: Regulatory Guide 43: Financial reporting and audit relief (reissued)
entries.summary: ASIC reissued Regulatory Guide 43 'Financial reporting and audit relief' on 24 April 2026, replacing the 2011 version and absorbing the now-withdrawn RG 29 (dual-listed company arrangements). RG 43 expressly notes that similar reporting and audit relief may be available for sustainability reports required under Chapter 2M, and directs entities seeking that relief to RG 280 'Sustainability reporting', particularly Section E - a direct cross-reference tying the general financial-reporting relief framework to the sustainability reporting regime already tracked by this case.
entries.verification_notes: Confirmed via two independent WebSearch queries: one corroborating the reissue date and RG 29 withdrawal/absorption, the other independently corroborating the specific Chapter 2M/RG 280 Section E cross-reference language. entries.document_title: How and when to lodge your sustainability report
entries.summary: ASIC's own guidance page setting the actual statutory lodgement deadlines for sustainability reports under s319 of the Corporations Act 2001: for Group 1 entities with a 30 June 2026 financial year end, listed entities must lodge their sustainability report (with the auditor's report) by 30 September 2026, and unlisted entities by 30 October 2026 - a three-to-four-month window after year-end, lodged alongside the annual financial report. This clarifies that the '30 June 2026' date referenced in ASIC's early-observations release (E-001/E-003) is the reporting period end date, not the lodgement deadline itself - non-lodgement enforcement for this first full Group 1 cohort cannot meaningfully begin until after these September/October 2026 deadlines pass.
entries.verification_notes: Verified via two independent WebSearch queries returning consistent statutory deadline dates (30 September 2026 listed / 30 October 2026 unlisted) and the s319 Corporations Act basis. This sandbox cannot fetch asic.gov.au directly.

proof_of_fact.rationale: Verified via independent WebSearch cross-reference only; no human/document-hash confirmation yet.

entries.document_title: 24-074MR Court finds Sunshine Loans charged customers prohibited fees
entries.summary: Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd is named in this document, fetched and independently verified by the DFAPTI target discovery script (dfapti/scripts/discover_targets.py) alongside a public-interest indicator: Federal Court found Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd contravened the National Credit Code and National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 by charging a prohibited $35 'amendment fee' on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts between 1 July 2016 and 2 November 2020, in proceedings brought by ASIC seeking $10.5 million in penalties. The company's application to have the trial judge recuse himself from the penalty hearing (on apprehended-bias grounds) was dismissed by the High Court of Australia on 18 March 2026 ([2026] HCA 8). Following the trial judge's recusal from the penalty phase (on apprehended-bias grounds relating to his credibility findings against Sunshine Loans and its witnesses), ASIC successfully appealed that recusal decision to the Full Federal Court (March 2025) and then, after Sunshine Loans' further appeal, to the High Court, which found unanimously (7-0) in ASIC's favour on 18 March 2026. The penalty hearing has now been remitted to the original trial judge, Justice Roger Derrington, for determination.
